- [WA] Cycle-length-only accounting: assume the answer is a sum of per-cycle formulas depending only on lengths, then ignore labels, orientation, and positions. Make it less brittle by solving tiny cycles exactly and trying a few anchors for large cycles, but keep cross-cycle effects out of the model.
- [WA] Independent-cycle solving: decompose the permutation and optimize each cycle as if operations, costs, or budgets never couple cycles. A realistic variant reconciles only parity or one representative per cycle, so simple decompositions pass while mixed cycles still break it.
- [WA] Cycle-choice knapsack compression: compute a small menu of costs or witnesses per cycle, then combine menus by sorting local deltas or spending a shared budget greedily. Exact menus for tiny cycles and parity reconciliation make it plausible, but an operation that touches two cycle representatives or a label-order constraint can make a locally second-best cycle choice globally necessary.
- [WA] Orientation-blind cycles: treat a cycle, its reverse, its rotation, or the inverse permutation as interchangeable. Try anchors at minimum index, minimum value, and first displaced point, then exact-score the output; it still fails when direction changes reachability or cost.
- [WA] Cycle-multiset canonicalization: sort cycles by length, minimum element, or chosen representative and solve that canonical order. Trying several deterministic orders hardens it, but original positions and interleavings remain erased.
- [WA] One-big-cycle extrapolation: derive a rule that is correct for a single cycle and apply it to arbitrary decompositions by summing, concatenating, or merging representatives. Exact handling for short cycles hides the flaw until long-plus-short mixtures matter.
- [WA] Parity-gated construction: use sign, cycle-count parity, inversion parity, or number of even cycles as the main feasibility gate, then fill the construction greedily. Parity-preserving repairs make it plausible, but the invariant is treated as nearly sufficient.
- [WA] Sparse-support shortcut: collect positions with `p[i] != i`, solve only that support, and treat fixed points as inert buffers. Clustered exact solves and dense fallbacks make it pass weak data while still ignoring fixed gaps that carry distance, crossing, or budget cost.
- [WA] Compressed gaps forgotten: compress moved positions to `1..k` and solve the induced permutation. Store coarse gap lengths or boundary parity to avoid toy failures, but omit absolute distances and crossings through fixed regions.
- [WA] Nearby-block independence: split displaced positions into intervals, value bands, or cycle clusters and solve each block separately. A boundary reconciliation pass helps, but the model cannot handle values or operation budget that must move through multiple blocks.
- [WA] Label-insensitive rank construction: use only relative order of values, not actual labels, endpoints, forbidden values, or special representatives. Extremal anchors and exact final scoring harden it without restoring label-specific constraints.
- [WA] Lehmer-code or inversion-table surrogate: replace the permutation by inversion counts, lexicographic rank, or another rank-code sequence and solve that encoded object. It is natural for order-statistic and lexicographic tasks and can decode a valid permutation, but it loses cycle adjacency, fixed-point gaps, original labels, and operation costs tied to positions rather than ranks.
- [WA] Canonical construction plus local repair: build one sorted, cycle-first, or left-greedy legal permutation and patch bad windows with adjacent swaps, short reversals, or local exact search. It resists small witnesses but never rebuilds from a different global cycle structure.
- [WA] Greedy cycle split/merge order: split the largest cycle, create the most fixed points, or merge cheapest-looking representatives first. Trying several representatives per cycle helps, but the approach still assumes split and merge order are locally exchangeable.
- [WA] Value-position symmetry overused: solve the inverse permutation, reversed positions, or reversed values and map back as if the objective were invariant under that view. A portfolio of views is a strong adversarial wrapper when the true cost is asymmetric.
- [WA] Monotone displacement matching: pair values to positions by closest distance, same displacement direction, or sorted deficit/surplus. Parity checks or cycle repair can patch legality, but nearest displacement is still mistaken for global optimality.
- [TLE] Long-cycle exactification: exact-solve each cycle by enumerating cuts, anchors, rotations, split points, or representative pairings because samples contain short cycles. A thresholded fallback passes weak tests, but one allowed `O(n)` cycle makes the exact branch visibly superlinear or factorial from the permutation model.
- [TLE] Dense move candidate evaluation: for every step, score all transpositions, reversals, swaps of representatives, or value-position pairings across the current permutation. Pruning by cycle membership, distance, or current proxy keeps moderate cases alive while missing moves outside the dense scan budget.
- [TLE] Repeated decomposition rebuilds: after each local edit, rebuild cycles, inverse positions, component labels, and all affected candidates from the full permutation. Small touched-region caches help on isolated swaps, but long cycles or many edits force `O(n)` reconstruction per move.
- [TLE] Adjacent-move trace simulation: represent a high-level permutation operation as many adjacent swaps and update inversions, breakpoints, or fixed points after each elementary move. This is plausible when statements describe swap sequences, but compact operations on long cycles expose the hidden operation-count blowup.
- [TLE] Branching tied to move degeneracy: keep every tied best move, every equivalent representative, or every cycle cut when legal moves grow with `n`. Fixed caps and signature deduplication turn it into WA; without them, symmetric permutations create a visible search blowup.
- [TLE] Subset transitions on moved support: after defining `k` as the number of displaced positions, enumerate subset transitions, subset endpoints, or pair masks on the moved support. It passes near-identity tests, then times out when `k` is linear even though the input is still just one permutation.
- [MLE] Bitmask DP on moved support: allocate `2^k`, `k * 2^k`, or pair-mask tables after assuming displaced support is naturally small. A cutoff that switches to cycle greedy avoids guaranteed crashes but turns the memory failure into a wrong heuristic on dense permutations.
- [MLE] Dense permutation relation storage: materialize an `n x n` reachability, crossing, swap-cost, or cycle-interaction table for a problem whose input is linear. Sparse cycle compression delays the failure while preserving the false assumption that omitted relations are irrelevant.
- [MLE] Storing generated permutations: BFS, beam search, or local search stores full arrays for every generated permutation state. Hashes, parent pointers, or compact signatures postpone memory failure, but many reachable states with the same coarse signature can need different futures.
- [MLE] Long-cycle variant cache: precompute and keep all rotations, cuts, normalized cycle views, or split products for each large cycle. This looks topic-specific and useful for exact scoring, but a single long cycle makes the cache quadratic or worse in stored permutation fragments.
- [MLE] Lazy update traces: store every delta, rollback record, and parent pointer for candidate permutations, expecting shared traces to stay shallow. Deep searches on one moved support or long cycle accumulate traces faster than states can be compacted, so memory fails without relying on container limits or random seeds.
- [RE] Recursive cycle traversal at linear depth: follow cycles or DFS the implicit permutation graph recursively with `n` allowed in the hundreds of thousands. Weak tests with short cycles pass; one valid long cycle makes recursion depth equal to the cycle length.
- [RE] Recursive search over cycle cuts: recurse once per chosen cut, representative, or support element while exactifying a cycle or moved block. Pruning handles tiny components, but monotone long-cycle instances can still create a call chain proportional to `n` before any branching is resolved.
- [RE] Lazy trace replay recursion: evaluate a generated permutation by recursively replaying parent deltas back to the base state. Beam or rollback paths that repeatedly touch the same long cycle create deep parent chains, so the crash follows from the search model rather than an arbitrary implementation bug.
